title: Arizona Public Records Request - Body Camera Footage, Stolen Car Suspect Chase and Fatal Shooting, South Phoenix, November 5, 2024

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Arizona Public Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

Under Arizona public records law, I request copies of records related to the following incident:

- Incident description: Stolen car suspect who was randomly firing a weapon near downtown Phoenix; officers pursued the vehicle; the chase ended when officers shot and killed the armed suspect in south Phoenix
- Incident date: November 5, 2024
- Location: South Phoenix, AZ

Please provide the following records in electronic format if available:
1. All body-worn camera (BWC) recordings from every officer involved in the pursuit or the shooting
2. All dashcam or in-car video associated with this incident
3. The incident report, offense report, and all supplemental or narrative reports
4. CAD logs, dispatch records, and call-for-service event history
5. Use-of-force documentation and any critical incident review records that are releasable
6. Any 911 audio or radio traffic recordings related to this event, to the extent releasable
7. Any index, retention schedule, or metadata identifying responsive video or audio files

If any responsive records are withheld or redacted in whole or in part, please identify the specific statutory exemption or privilege asserted for each withheld item and release all reasonably segregable non-exempt portions.

If fees are expected to exceed $25.00, please provide a written cost estimate prior to processing.

Please confirm receipt and provide any request or tracking number.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

Sincerely,

David McCriston

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Tom Nash, a citizen of Virginia, in coordination with Alan J. Segal.

communications.communication:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

On April 4, 2026 at 04:52:52.761Z, a Flock Safety query (eadff215-bbc2-4522-82b7-9c3d05e2d07d) was made on case 26-0000 of 644 unique Flock Safety systems for "Obstructing Justice" 

For this query, please provide:
1) Police Report, Case Records, CAD Notes, Case Notes, or other comments or descriptions related to the case, search, or explanation of how justice was being obstructed and how ALPR data aided in an investigation
2) There are dozens of queries listed as 26-0000, please provide any information related to case 26-0000, or any department policy or guidance that states when this case number should be used for queries (e.g. as a placeholder)

Thank you for the submission. However, there are a few issues that need to be addressed before it could be processed. First, we need a custodian for these:

  *   All communications received from any representative or employee of TikTok
  *   All notifications provided by any representative or employee of TikTok, including any Incident Response Specialists.

The custodian could be an OSTP personnel (for instance, OSTP’s current director, Michael Kratsios) or a named TikTok representative.
Second, there are two response dates noted therein:

  *   “I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.”
  *   “I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 15 business days, as the statute requires.”

Neither is correct, unless the former is meant to be a request for expedited access, in which case, the request would have to show that there’s imminent threat to an individual or that you are a person primarily engaged in information dissemination and meet the other requisites for such processing. Absent those prerequisites, an agency is required to respond within 20 business days.

Third, we need a date range for these.

Please address the above issues and resubmit the request at your earliest convenience.
Regards,

OSTP FOIA Office.

Syllabi Requirements: Any standardized syllabi language or templates mandated or recommended by the School regarding the submission of student work to third-party AI detection platforms.

Privacy and Security: Any Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) or FERPA-compliance reviews specific to the School’s use of these vendors, particularly regarding the storage and processing of student-generated data.

Accuracy and Efficacy: Any internal reports or assessments regarding the accuracy, "false positive" rates, or ethical reviews of AI detection tools currently utilized within this School.

title: Public Records Request — St. Paul Generative AI Records

communications.subject: Minnesota Government Data Practices Act Request: Public Records Request — St. Paul Generative AI Records
communications.communication:
Subject: Public Records Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 17, 2026

Data Practices Officer
City of St. Paul
15 Kellogg Blvd. West
St. Paul, MN 55102

Dear Data Practices Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to the City of St. Paul's use of generative AI tools by city employees.

We are requesting the following records under Minn. Stat. § 13.03:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by city employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing city employee use of ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for any generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

Minnesota IT Services (MNIT) rolled out Microsoft Copilot Chat to over 22,000 state and local government employees in April 2025. St. Paul city employees may have access to Copilot through the state's enterprise deployment or through separate city IT provisioning. Minnesota police departments have also been increasingly adopting AI tools for report writing and other tasks (see MPR News, "An AI tool is generating police reports for some Minnesota departments," Oct. 8, 2025, https://www.mprnews.org/story/2025/10/08/minnesota-police-departments-using-ai-to-generate-reports).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console, the Purview compliance center, or individual user account settings. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Minnesota Government Data Practices Act, all government data collected, created, received, maintained, or disseminated by a government entity is presumed public unless classified otherwise by statute. Minn. Stat. §§ 13.01, 13.03. Governor Walz's Executive Order 24-03 (2024) directed state agencies to develop policies for responsible AI use including transparency and accountability. Minnesota IT Services (MNIT) has issued guidance requiring that AI-generated content used in official business be treated as government data subject to existing retention schedules. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

April 9, 2026

Public Records Officer
Indiana Office of Technology
100 N. Senate Avenue, Room N551
Indianapolis, IN 46204

Dear Public Records Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to Indiana's use of generative AI tools by state employees.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, Google Gemini, "Captain Record," "Ask Indiana," or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by state employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing state employee use of ChatGPT, Google Gemini, "Captain Record," "Ask Indiana," or other generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.

4. Administrative usage statistics for any generative AI chatbot tools showing number of users, sessions, and queries processed.

We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

The Indiana Secretary of State's Office launched "Captain Record," a generative AI tool built on Google Cloud's Vertex AI and Gemini 2.0, enabling users to search more than 100 years of state documents using natural language queries (see StateScoop, "Indiana launches 'Captain Record,' the AI tool that searches 100 years of state documents," https://statescoop.com/indiana-captain-record-ai-search-tool/). The state also launched the "Ask Indiana" public-facing AI chatbot on IN.gov (see Indiana Capital Chronicle, "Meet 'Ask Indiana,' state government's new AI chatbot," May 15, 2025, https://indianacapitalchronicle.com/2025/05/15/meet-ask-indiana-state-governments-new-ai-resident-assistant-chatbot/). Additionally, the Secretary of State's Office partnered with Google to launch "Liz" and "Otto," AI chatbots supporting the INBiz platform and auto dealer licensing system, respectively. Indiana's AI Policy and Guidance is published at https://www.in.gov/mph/AI/.

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Google Gemini-based tools such as "Captain Record," logs may be available through Google Cloud administrative consoles or server-side conversation storage. For "Ask Indiana," logs may be available through the platform's administration tools. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under Indiana's Access to Public Records Act, IC 5-14-3, public records include "any writing, paper, report, study, map, photograph, book, card, tape recording, or other material that is created, received, retained, maintained, or filed by or with a public agency." IC 5-14-3-2(q). AI chatbot interaction logs created or used by state employees in the course of their official duties qualify as public records under this definition. Indiana's published AI Policy and Guidance further confirms that these AI tools are part of official state operations. Additionally, NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02, provides persuasive authority that chats and electronic messages used for government business are likely government records.

1. All interaction logs from ChatGPT, ChatGPT Enterprise, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, and any other generative AI chatbot tools used by EOIR employees (including immigration judges, Board members, law clerks, and administrative staff) — including prompts submitted by employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from April 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. 3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.


We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

Dan Bateyko
PhD Student
Cornell University
drb348@cornell.edu

communications.subject: Request Acknowledgement by DOJ-EOIR PAL
communications.communication:
Dear Dan  Bateyko, 

This response acknowledges receipt of your Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request by the Executive Office for Immigration Review (EOIR).  Your request has been assigned control number 2026-36721. 

Please be advised that the status of requests submitted through the Public Access Link (PAL) may be tracked at https://foia.eoir.justice.gov/app/Home.aspx, and you will receive an email response to your request upon completion of processing. The status of requests submitted by email or mail may not be trackable through the PAL, but you will still receive an email or mailed response to your request upon completion of processing. (https://foia.eoir.justice.gov/app/Home.aspx) Please ensure the email address you provided in PAL will be able to receive the messages we send you. Messages rejected by your email account will not be rerouted to you. 
Please be aware that EOIR is a component of the Department of Justice and the custodian of records for the “Record of Proceedings” (ROP), which is the compilation of documents and digital audio recordings produced in proceedings before the immigration courts and the BIA. The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) is the custodian of records for the “A-file,” which is a series of records of a person’s immigration history and is maintained and stored by DHS. EOIR is not the custodian of records for DHS or its components, nor does EOIR have access to DHS’s A-files. EOIR does not create an ROP until matters are initiated by DHS. Nevertheless, EOIR may create and maintain ROPs for proceedings other than removal proceedings (i.e., credible fear reviews, bond hearings). You may request a copy of your Notice to Appear (NTA) and full A-file from DHS. (https://www.dhs.gov/foia) 
Note that parties to immigration cases (including respondents and their representatives of record) have the option to request their Record of Proceedings directly from the immigration court or Board of Immigration Appeals. Please see the instructions here: https://www.justice.gov/eoir/ROPrequest. (https://www.justice.gov/eoir/ROPrequest) 

If you have filed a fee waiver request, EOIR will address the fee waiver in a separate letter. Most requests for records of proceedings do not require any fees; however, if the fees for processing the request are estimated to exceed $25.00, EOIR will notify you before processing the request to determine whether you will commit to paying the fee or whether you wish to narrow the scope of your request to reduce the fee. Fees may be charged for searching records at the rate of $4.75 (administrative)/$10.00 (professional) per quarter hour, and for duplication of copies at the rate of $.05 per copy. The first 100 copies and two hours of search time are not charged, and charges must exceed $25.00 before we will charge a fee. 

The FOIA requires an agency to respond within 20 working days after receipt of the request, and EOIR endeavors to meet this standard.  The FOIA permits a ten-day extension of this time period, pursuant to 5 U.S.C. § 552(a)(6)(B), based on unusual circumstances.  Your request involves “unusual circumstances,” and EOIR is extending the time period to respond by an additional 10 working days because your request requires the collection of records from field offices, or involves a search, collection, and review of voluminous records, or requires consultation with another agency or two or more agency components. 

EOIR FOIA requests are placed in one of three tracks. Track one is for those requests that seek and receive expedited processing pursuant to subsection (a)(6)(E) of the FOIA. Track two is for simple requests that do not involve voluminous records or lengthy consultations with other entities.  Track three is for complex requests that involve voluminous records and for which lengthy or numerous consultations are required, or those requests that may involve sensitive records.  As a matter of default, your request has been placed in track two for simple requests.  If you have requested expedited processing, EOIR will contact you in a separate letter. 

If you have any questions regarding unusual circumstances, you may contact the EOIR FOIA Service Center or FOIA Public Liaison at EOIR.FOIArequests@usdoj.gov to discuss reformulation or an alternative time frame to process your request, or for any further assistance regarding any aspect of your request. Alternatively, you may contact the Office of Government Information Services (OGIS) at the National Archives and Records Administration to inquire about the FOIA mediation services they offer.  The contact information for OGIS is as follows: Office of Government Information Services, National Archives and Records Administration, Room 2510, 8601 Adelphi Road, College Park, Maryland 20740-6001; e-mail at ogis@nara.gov; telephone at (202) 741-5770; toll free at (877) 684-6448; or facsimile at (202) 741-5769. 
 
Sincerely, 

FOIA Intake
